coredata.dc:title: Preparation and optical properties of angle-dependent photonic crystals based on multi-layer films
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Qingfei
Huang, Lu
Shi, Weimin
Zhu, Yubin
Wang, Linjun
coredata.dc:description: The CrSiO2 photonic crystals, featured with two-dimensional structure and optical variation, was prepared by magnetron sputtering and electron beam lithography method. The finite difference time domain method (FDTD) is used to study the light-modulated characteristics of photonic crystals. Hue difference (⊿H) was used to evaluate the optical variations of photonic crystals, and bigger ⊿H indicates stronger angle dependency of color variation. Various parameters are analyzed for their impact upon ⊿H, including hole geometry (radium, r), hole gap (distance between the centers of two circles, d) and hole depth (h). The results matched the ones from simulation. Carbon quantum dots and up-conversion nanocrystals NaYF4 were inserted into the photonic crystals, and testing results of these doped crystals showed optical variation of fluorescence in the UV and IR region.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photonic crystal
Optical variability
Finite difference time domain
Multi-layer films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The modeling of incident and observing angles measured by BYK-mac spectrometer.

Reflective spectrum of simulated samples for photonic crystals (a) the same hole gap, the same hole depth and different hole radius (r = 150 nm、250 nm、400 nm) (b) the same radius, the same depth, and different gap (d = 1.1 μm、1.5 μm) (c) the same gap, the same radius and different depth (h = 200 nm、300 nm、600 nm、800 nm).
Surface topography is measured by SEM (a) top view of sample (b) cross section of structure (c) schematic structure of multi-layer films.
The optical phenomenon of ridescent colors on 2D photonic crystals. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
SEM images of fluorescent fill-up by (a) carbon quantum dots and (b) up-conversion nanocrystals NaYF4.
Fluorescent spectra under two angles for the doped photonic crystals of (a) carbon quantum dots (excitation wavelength in 380 nm) and (b) nanocrystals NaYF4 (excitation wavelength in 980 nm).
Caculating optical parameters on different hole radius (r) by datacolor TOOLS.
Caculating optical parameters under different hole gap (d) by datacolor TOOLS.
Caculating optical parameters under different hole depth (h) by datacolor TOOLS.
Testing optical parameters on different angle by multi-angle reflective spetroscope.

head.ce:title: Preparation and optical properties of angle-dependent photonic crystals based on multi-layer films

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai, 200444, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The CrSiO2 photonic crystals, featured with two-dimensional structure and optical variation, was prepared by magnetron sputtering and electron beam lithography method. The finite difference time domain method (FDTD) is used to study the light-modulated characteristics of photonic crystals. Hue difference (⊿H) was used to evaluate the optical variations of photonic crystals, and bigger ⊿H indicates stronger angle dependency of color variation. Various parameters are analyzed for their impact upon ⊿H, including hole geometry (radium, r), hole gap (distance between the centers of two circles, d) and hole depth (h). The results matched the ones from simulation. Carbon quantum dots and up-conversion nanocrystals NaYF4 were inserted into the photonic crystals, and testing results of these doped crystals showed optical variation of fluorescence in the UV and IR region.

ce:section.ce:para: Photonic crystal [1], a novel structured raw materials, whose dielectric coefficient will change periodically based on dimension, is the hot domain in recent scientific advances. The Bragg scattering and interference brought by lattice periodicality, will create the so-called photonic band gap (PBG) within the material, which will block the entrance of photos within the wavelength. Due to its capacity to control light transmission, photonic crystal has a wide range of applications, such as optical fiber, light guidance [2], LED, optical filter, solar cells [3–6], full light switch, sensing [7–9]and optical anti-counterfeiting. For applications in optical anti-counterfeiting, research of photonic crystal [10–15] focuses on one-dimension or multi-layer structured films, which include deflection, interference and diffraction for optical security.

Experimental preparation of multi-layer films photonic crystal with metal layer and substrate layer is reported in this paper. Because the multi-layer films structured photonic crystal of CrSiO2 Cr has also been done in previous research, the two dimensional (2D) photonic crystals of CrSiO2 deposited on Si substrate are novel structure. With reflective metal layer of Cr and substrate layer of SiO2, multi-layer films are acquired by magnetron sputtering, periodical holes are punched by e-beam lithography, resulting in 2D photonic crystals. Hole radius, hole gap and their depth are altered, so as that their impact on angle dependency, as well as their influence on lattice parameters could be optically simulated by FDTD. To further understand invisible light transmission in the photonic crystals, carbon quantum dots [16], and up-conversion NaYF4 nano-particles [17–19]are used to fill the internal holes, so as to further explore the angle dependency of photonic crystal by fluorescence excited by UV and IR light region.

Materials and Instruments: Silicon wafer (base substrate), Alcohol 75%, Target plate Cr (99.99%), Target plate Si (99.99%), Vacuum Heater, Magnetron sputtering, BYK-mac multi-angle spectrophotometer, Scanning Electron Microscope Hitachi SU3500.

A layer of Cr, with a thickness of 10 nm, is deposited on the silicon substrate, and another layer of SiO2 is deposited above with a thickness of 600 nm. Columns of holes are regularly prepared by e-beam lithography. The array holes are 400 nm in radius, 1.6 μm in gap, and 400 nm in depth. The first step is to get Cr deposited on the surface. It is processed in a chamber with a pressure of 0.5Pa, where argon is sputtered for 40 s with a power of 15 W at a flow rate of 10 sccm. Then SiO2 is deposited above. It is processed in a chamber filled with argon as the sputtering gas and oxygen as the reactive gas. The chamber pressured at 0.5Pa is kept with a vacuum of 6.4 × 10−4Pa, where sputtering is controlled for 170 min with a power of 230 W. The ratio of O2 (8 sccm) to Argon (20 sccm) is 2:5. Angle dependency is measured by BYK-mac multi-angle reflective spetroscope. To further explore angle dependency of photonic crystal, carbon quantum dots [20] (5% water solution) and NaYF4:Yb, Er (5% cyclo-hexane solution) are used to fill up the nano-holes. In vacuum pressure (<10−3 Pa), the photonic crystals are infiltrated with nano-particles fluorescent solution drop by drop, and then dried solution completely at room temperature in vacuum. The infiltration time of 5% water solution by carbon quantum dots needs 20 min completely. And 5% cyclo-hexane solution by NaYF4:Yb, Er is infiltrated entirely by 2 min. To ensure enough nano-particles filling the holes, this process is repeated many times. Finally, 75% alcohol solution is used to wipe off the fluorescent nano-particles on the surface, photonic crystals filled with fluorescent nano-particles are acquired.

In this paper, the incident light is considered unpolarized. To measure angle dependency, the wavelength between 0.4 μm and 0.7 μm is chosen under two angles of 0° and 60° respectively and are used to analyze reflectance curve. Accordingly when BYK-mac spectrometer is used shown in Fig. 1 , its incident angle is 45° from the surface of the sample. Thus 45° and −15° angles are chosen as the observing angles in spectrometer corresponding to observing angles of 0° and 60° from normal in simulation modeling. L, a, b, and C would be calculated based on the measurements for these two angles, the hue difference (⊿H) is further calculated using Eq. (1), which reflects directly the capacity of angle dependency.
[1]
Δ H =
[
(
Δ a
)
2
+
(
Δ b
)
2
−
(
Δ c
)
2
]
Using FDTD Solutions, photonic crystals with various parameters of hole radius (r), gap (d), and depth (h) are designed to simulate.

Fig. 2 (a) shows reflectance curve under two angles for photonic crystals with the same hole gap and the same hole depth. Reflectance changes significantly for two angles with different hole radius, meaning the photonic crystal will shift color regardless of hole radius. At angle of 0°, hole radius hardly has any effect upon reflectance curve, which means hole radius will hardly impact vertically observed color. In the same time, reflectance curve will be strongly impacted by hole radius for 60°. Inputting reflectance measurements, datacolor TOOLS will help to calculate L, a, b and C for different hole radius, and come the resulting ⊿H in Table 1 . The highest ⊿H at r = 150 nm indicates the best optical variability.

Reflective spectra are simulated for samples with the same radius, the same depth, and different gap. For vertical observation, gap difference hardly influences the color, and no evident color change under 60°. From Fig. 2(b), reflective curve moves to the red direction with increased gap for 0°, while the reflective curve moves to the blue direction for 60°, which could be seen by ⊿H calculations. The change of gap is a direct impact on lattice constant in the photonic crystal, which means larger lattice constant will bring up stronger angle dependency.

Inputing reflectance results, L, a, b, and C are calculated by datacolor TOOLS. Subsequently, ⊿H will be given under two angles shown in Table 2 . Comparing the results between the gap of 1.5 μm and 1.1 μm, the former has a slightly better color shifting effect.

Reflectance spectra are simulated for samples with the same gap, the same radius and different depth in Fig. 2(c). Higher impact is observed when angle is under 0°. As the depth increases, reflective peak for long wavelength under 0° remains the same with narrower frequency. And at the same time, the reflective peak for short wavelength is moving toward the red direction with shorter peak, which means purer color and higher saturation. When angle is under 60°, the result displays unnoticeable color change. It could be deduced that larger depth/radius ratio will enhance color purity with a single hue. The ⊿H is obtained by using datacolor TOOLS shown in Table 3 . The ⊿H is biggest, which means the most distinct optical variability, when h is at 200 nm.

Surface topography is measured by SEM. Fig. 3 (a) is the top view of samples, while Fig. 3(b) is the cross section of structure. The radius of the surface holes is 400 nm with a gap of 1500 nm. These holes are aligned in square with smooth surface and no noticeable flaw. The depth is 200 nm shown in the cross-section picture. Energy loss of electronic beam increases as hole deepens. In the same time, hole radius decreases slightly, which resulting in a hole of cone rather than in the form of column. The schematic structure of multi-layer films is demonstrated in Fig. 3(c). The results are shown under different angles in Table 4 .

The comparison of luminescence excited by visible light between 2D photonic crystals and reference sample deposited on a uniform SiO2 substrate has been displayed Fig. 4 . It is apparently shown optical phenomenon of ridescent colors in the region of 2D photonic crystals, but it doesn't exist in the other region of reference sample deposited on a uniform SiO2 substrate. As a result, The ⊿H is 55.4 indicating evident angle dependency, which changes from yellow to blue based on Lab value, thus comforming with simulated result.

To further understand angle dependency, carbon quantum dot [21] and up-conversion nanocrystal NaYF4 are used respectively to fill the holes in the photonic crystals by liquid growth under vacuum environment. Carbon quantum dots almost completely fills the pores displayed in Fig. 5 (a), while nanocrystal NaYF4 just precipitated on the inner surface of tube shown in Fig. 5(b) as cyclo-hexane evaporates too quickly.

Fluorescent spectra under two angles for the doped photonic crystals are unified in terms of strength. Carbon quantum dots are excited fluorescent wavelength of 380 nm In Fig. 6 (a) and NaYF4 nanocrystals are at wavelength of 980 nm in Fig. 6(b). The 0° incident angle excited by wavelength of 380 nm and 980 nm is vertical to the surface of the sample. And two angles of observing fluorescence luminescence are chosen from 0° to 60° respectively [22]. It could be seen that, as the observed angle tilts, the excited peak of the fluorescent spectrum is moving toward the blue direction, and this also happened for visible light source, which indicated angle dependent characteristics. It is shown by the experiment that the existence of PBG will invoke the angular adjustment of excited light source. Subsequently angle dependency will take effect, providing inspiration for development of angle dependent fluorescent materials.

Angle dependency of photonic crystals, which is deeply impacted by various parameters, is studied in this paper based on plane wave theory along with hue difference. Two dimensional photonic crystals, perfectly correlating theoretical calculations with experimental measurements, are prepared by magnetron sputtering and e-beam lithography. Photonic crystals show strong angle dependency of color variations, which are changing from yellow to blue with a ⊿H of 55.4 between angles at 0° and 60°. When inserted with carbon quantum dots and up-conversion nanocrystals of NaYF4, photonic crystals also feature angle dependent characteristics in the UV and IR spectra. While the observed angle tilts, the fluorescent spectra are moving toward the direction of blue shift.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the project funded by Nature Science Foundation of Shanghai (Grant no. 18ZR1415600). They also thank Mr Yuliang Chu (Analysis and Testing Center of Shanghai University) and Xiangyun Song (Energy Storage and Distributed Resources Division, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory) for their kind assistance with SEM measurements.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: A. Chiasera, C. Meroni, F. Scotognella, et al., Opt. Mater. In Press.
